GEOG771 Assignment 1

Benjamin Preston

2026-03-08

Part 1

Q1.

Make 3 histograms for data and variables of your choice. Make sure the graphs have titles, are of different colours and have the axis labeled. For the same variables chosen to look at the distributions provide some descriptive statistics. Put the code, results and the exported graphs into an Rmarkdown document.

census_small <- read_csv("Lab01/data/census_small.csv")
glimpse(census_small)
## Rows: 30
## Columns: 5
## $ Code                         <chr> "E05000886", "E05000887", "E05000888", "E…
## $ Ward                         <chr> "Allerton and Hunts Cross", "Anfield", "B…
## $ PCT_Good_Health              <dbl> 48.97327, 42.20538, 40.84911, 58.62832, 5…
## $ PCT_Higher_Managerial        <dbl> 10.091491, 2.912621, 3.931920, 7.019923, …
## $ PCT_Social_Rented_Households <dbl> 13.005190, 22.772576, 42.555119, 18.36391…
census_small %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = PCT_Good_Health)) +
  geom_histogram(fill = "blue",
                 color = "blue",
                 alpha = 0.2,
                 binwidth = 5) +
  labs(title = "Small Census - Percentage in Good Health", 
       x = "PCT_Good_Health (%)", 
       y = "Number of Boroughs (n)")

# Summary Stats for PCT_Good_Health
census_small %>%
  summarise(mean = mean(PCT_Good_Health, na.rm = TRUE),
            median = median(PCT_Good_Health, na.rm = TRUE),
            sd = sd(PCT_Good_Health, na.rm = TRUE),
            min = min(PCT_Good_Health, na.rm = TRUE),
            max = max(PCT_Good_Health, na.rm = TRUE),
            n = n()
            )
## # A tibble: 1 × 6
##    mean median    sd   min   max     n
##   <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <int>
## 1  46.7   46.4  4.72  37.3  58.6    30

Q2.

Read in the csv or the RData version of the dataset Deprivation_Scotland_2011DZ_complete. How many observations and variables are in the dataset, what variables are included in the dataset and how are they coded (types of variables)? Incorporate the code and the responses in your rmarkdown.

dep_data <- read_csv("Lab01/data/Deprivation_Scotland_2011DZ_complete.csv")
## Rows: 6500 Columns: 92
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## chr (12): Datazone, Health.Board, Council.Area, umemp.group, region, region2...
## dbl (80): Pop.total, Pop.hh, Pop.comm, Quintile, Decile, DEPCAT, Most.dep.15...
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.

There are 6500 observations and 92 variables in the dataset. 12 of the variables are character variables, 80 are numeric (double) variables. The variables include demographic information, deprivation indices, and urban-rural classifications for datazones in Scotland.

Q3.

In Deprivation_Scotland_2011DZ_complete the variables no_qual_M_25_64, no_qual_F_25_64 and no_qual_T_25_64 give the number of men, women and total population aged 25-64 with no educational qualifications. Calculate the percent of men, women and total population in that age range with no qualifications. Use the variables all_M_25_64, all_F_25_64 and all_T_25_64 as denominators. How many datazones (spatial administrative unit in Scotland) have over 20% of people aged 25-64 with no qualifications?

dep_data <- dep_data %>%
  mutate(pct_no_qual_M_25_64 = (no_qual_M_25_64 / all_M_25_64) * 100,
         pct_no_qual_F_25_64 = (no_qual_F_25_64 / all_F_25_64) * 100,
         pct_no_qual_T_25_64 = (no_qual_T_25_64 / all_T_25_64) * 100)

datazones_over_20pct <- dep_data %>% 
  filter(pct_no_qual_T_25_64 > 20) %>%
  nrow() %>%  print()
## [1] 3242

There are 3242 datazones in Scotland where over 20% of people aged 25-64 have no educational qualifications. That is ~50% of the 6500 datazones in the dataset.

Q4.

Summarize the variable UR8FOLD. What type is this variable? Create an ordered factor variable of the 8-fold urban-rural classification. The description of the categories is in the file SGUR2011_2012_UR8FOLD_CodeDesc.txt. Try to use short but descriptive variable labels. To learn more about UR8FOLD (2016 Urban Rural Classification. Classification identifying urban and rural areas based on settlement size and drive times) go to https://www.gov.scot/publications/scottish-government-urban-rural-classification-2016/pages/6.

dep_data <- dep_data %>%
  mutate(UR8FOLD_factor = factor(UR8FOLD, 
                                 levels = 1:8, 
                                 labels = c("Large Urban",
                                            "Other Urban",
                                            "Accessible Small Town",
                                            "Remote Small Town",
                                            "Accessible Rural",
                                            "Remote Rural",
                                            "Very Remote Rural",
                                            "Island"
                                            )
                                 )
         )

dep_data %>%
  count(UR8FOLD_factor) %>%
  bind_rows(summarise(., UR8FOLD_factor = "Total", n = sum(n)))
## # A tibble: 9 × 2
##   UR8FOLD_factor            n
##   <chr>                 <int>
## 1 Large Urban            2452
## 2 Other Urban            2035
## 3 Accessible Small Town   583
## 4 Remote Small Town       160
## 5 Accessible Rural         95
## 6 Remote Rural            738
## 7 Very Remote Rural       220
## 8 Island                  217
## 9 Total                  6500

Q5.

Create a 3-fold urban-rural classification (from the 8-fold) that groups datazones into accessible areas (including urban areas), remote areas and very remote areas.

dep_data <- dep_data %>%
  mutate(UR3FOLD_factor = case_when(
                      UR8FOLD_factor %in% c("Large Urban", 
                          "Other Urban", 
                          "Accessible Rural",
                          "Accessible Small Town") ~ "Accessible",
                      UR8FOLD_factor %in% c("Remote Small Town", 
                          "Remote Rural") ~ "Remote",
                      UR8FOLD_factor %in% c("Very Remote Rural", 
                          "Island") ~ "Very Remote",
                      TRUE ~ NA_character_
                      )
  )

dep_data %>%
  count(UR3FOLD_factor) %>%
  bind_rows(summarise(., UR3FOLD_factor = "Total", n = sum(n)))
## # A tibble: 4 × 2
##   UR3FOLD_factor     n
##   <chr>          <int>
## 1 Accessible      5165
## 2 Remote           898
## 3 Very Remote      437
## 4 Total           6500

Part 2

Data for Part 2 of assignment will be available to download from canvas. You can make all the maps using tmap, or use a mix of different packages. The choice is yours.

Q1.

Make a population density map of the world.

  • use library library(sf) to load COUNTRIES_ALL shapefile as Countries (you can name this dataset as you wish).

  • some of the rows in the dataset have NA values - in order to delete them make a subset of Countries with rows where Pop2015 is higher than 0 (or eliminate rows with NA values).

  • you do not have a variable Population density 2015 in the Countries or Countries2 datasets. You need to create it by adding a new variable POP_DENS. Population density equals a total number of people divided by squared area POP2015/SQKM.

  • create a choropleth map showing population density in the countries of the world.

  • use 7 classes to visualise your population density into classes.

  • look at the “classInt” or “tmap” tm_scale_intervals documentation in order to identify the best classification method. The best will be the one which shows your pattern in the clearest way.

  • you can use RColorBrewer library in order to identify the scheme of colours of your choice or tmap brewer choices.

Countries <- st_read("Lab01/Data_assignment/COUNTRIES_ALL.shp")
## Reading layer `COUNTRIES_ALL' from data source 
##   `/Users/bpreston/Library/CloudStorage/GoogleDrive-bhp1972@gmail.com/My Drive/_bhp1972/Study/Auckland/GEOG771/Lab01/Data_assignment/COUNTRIES_ALL.shp' 
##   using driver `ESRI Shapefile'
## Simple feature collection with 247 features and 7 fields
## Geometry type: MULTIPOLYGON
## Dimension:     XY
## Bounding box:  xmin: -180 ymin: -58.49861 xmax: 180 ymax: 83.6236
## Geodetic CRS:  WGS 84
Countries <- Countries %>%
  filter(POP2015 > 0) %>%
  mutate(POP_DENS = POP2015 / SQKM,
         LOG2_POP_DENS = log2(POP_DENS),
         LOG2_POP_DENS_CLASS = cut(LOG2_POP_DENS, 
                                   breaks = 7,
                                   na.rm = TRUE,
                                   labels = c("Very Low", 
                                              "Low", 
                                              "Low-Med", 
                                              "Med", 
                                              "Med-High", 
                                              "High", 
                                              "Very High"),
                                   include.lowest = TRUE
         )
  )
         
# Plot with classified fill
Countries %>% ggplot() +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = LOG2_POP_DENS_CLASS), colour = "white", linewidth = 0.1) +
  scale_fill_brewer(
    palette = "Blues",
    name = "Population Density",
    na.value = "grey80"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "World Population Density",
    subtitle = "People per square kilometre (7 classes)",
    caption = "Source: COUNTRIES_ALL shapefile"
  ) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5, colour = "grey40"),
    plot.caption = element_text(size = 8, colour = "grey60"),
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.title = element_text(size = 9),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 8)
  )

Note that I have used a log2 transformation of population density to better visualize the wide range of values across countries. The classes are based on the log-transformed population density, which helps to show population densities more clearly. I have not used the classInt package for classification, but instead used the base R cut() function to create 7 classes.

Q2.

Make a map of China with Chinese big cities

  • make a subset of dataset Countries where you choose only one county - China

  • use library library(sf) to load cities file. You can name it cities

  • in order to choose only Chinese cities you can use raster library and “intersect” command. You can also use st_intersecting as we did in the tutorials.

  • Name the new dataset chinese_cities

cities <- st_read("Lab01/Data_assignment/cities.shp")
## Reading layer `cities' from data source 
##   `/Users/bpreston/Library/CloudStorage/GoogleDrive-bhp1972@gmail.com/My Drive/_bhp1972/Study/Auckland/GEOG771/Lab01/Data_assignment/cities.shp' 
##   using driver `ESRI Shapefile'
## Simple feature collection with 48 features and 8 fields
## Geometry type: POINT
## Dimension:     XY
## Bounding box:  xmin: -118.25 ymin: -33.47502 xmax: 139.809 ymax: 59.91666
## Geodetic CRS:  WGS 84
# Make sure both layers use the same CRS
cities <- st_transform(cities, st_crs(Countries))

# Filter to Chinese cities using st_intersects
china <- Countries %>% filter(CNTRY_NAME == "China")

# Get cities that intersect with China
chinese_cities <- cities[st_intersects(cities, china, sparse = FALSE), ]

# Verify results
glimpse(chinese_cities)
## Rows: 8
## Columns: 9
## $ OBJECTID_1 <dbl> 35, 36, 37, 38, 39, 40, 41, 44
## $ OBJECTID   <dbl> 35, 36, 37, 38, 39, 40, 41, 44
## $ CITY_NAME  <chr> "Chongqing", "Shanghai", "Wuhan", "Guangzhou", "Shenyang", …
## $ FIPS_CNTRY <chr> "CH", "CH", "CH", "CH", "CH", "CH", "CH", "CH"
## $ CNTRY_NAME <chr> "China", "China", "China", "China", "China", "China", "Chin…
## $ POINT_X    <dbl> 106.5227, 121.4730, 114.2790, 113.2936, 123.3831, 116.3880,…
## $ POINT_Y    <dbl> 29.54410, 31.24800, 30.57300, 23.09619, 41.80217, 39.90619,…
## $ POPULATION <dbl> 3934200, 9838400, 4488900, 4154800, 3981000, 7441000, 50959…
## $ geometry   <POINT [°]> POINT (106.5227 29.5441), POINT (121.473 31.248), POINT (11…

  • plot a map of China with a background colour of your choice

  • plot Chinese cities on the top map of China.You can decide what colour and size the symbols will be. Make sure they are visible on your final map.

  • add labels to the cities. Use CITY_NAME variable for this purpose.

  • add legend and a title to your map

# Add coordinates for ggrepel
chinese_cities_coords <- chinese_cities %>%
  mutate(lon = st_coordinates(.)[,1],
         lat = st_coordinates(.)[,2])

# Create plot
ggplot() +
  # Set sea/background colour
  theme(panel.background = element_rect(fill = "#ACD8E5")) +
  
  # Plot China polygon
  geom_sf(data = china, fill = "#90C97E", colour = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +
  
  # Plot Chinese cities
  geom_sf(data = chinese_cities, colour = "red", size = 1, alpha = 0.7) +
  
  # Add labels for cities
  geom_text_repel(data = chinese_cities_coords,
                aes(x = lon, y = lat, label = CITY_NAME),
                size = 2.5,
                colour = "black",
                max.overlaps = 20) +
  
  # Add other labels and title
  labs(
    title = "Cities of China",
    caption = "Source: COUNTRIES_ALL & cities shapefiles"
  ) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "#ACD8E5"),  # sea blue background
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),
    plot.caption = element_text(size = 8, colour = "grey60"),
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "#ACD8E5", colour = NA)
  )
